---
title: "VI — Product realization"
description: "Turning a circuit into reproducible, testable, supportable hardware."
page-role: part
part-id: realization
---

A working prototype is evidence, not yet a product. Product realization makes
requirements, interfaces, layout, manufacture, test, compliance, service, and
retirement part of the design rather than paperwork added at the end.

## Part question

How does an electronic design become hardware that another person can build,
verify, operate, maintain, and retire under explicit constraints?

## Entry knowledge

This part is most useful alongside a concrete circuit or embedded design. It
assumes measurement and debugging from F06, relevant analog and digital
behavior, and the evidence-reading practices in [T01](../appendices/t01-datasheets-standards.qmd).
R01 can be read early; R02–R06 draw on increasingly broad technical experience.

## Learning arc

| Chapters | Development |
|---|---|
| R01 | turn needs into bounded requirements, architecture, interfaces, and recorded trade-offs |
| R02–R04 | capture a design, control layout interactions, release it for manufacture, and bring it up in stages |
| R05 | design calibration, reliability evidence, and production tests around decisions |
| R06 | integrate safety, security, compliance, maintenance, and end-of-life obligations |

## Exit capability

You should be able to create a traceable baseline from requirement through
verification, release recoverable design and manufacturing sources, isolate a
bring-up failure without compounding it, and state which lifecycle claims the
evidence does—and does not—support.

Labs L15, L19, and L20 exercise bring-up, repeat build, and qualification. The
projects introduce these practices early and revisit them at higher consequence;
see the [practice and project spine](../roadmap.qmd#practice-and-project-spine).
